sentences.text: Yeah.
As I said, my grandma came to live with us.
My mother tried to do something for her and failed.
And on August 15, 1942, the Gestapo came and they took my grandma.
Now, during that week, I have to mention, my uncle Paul, her son Paul, had died.
And we tried to hide it from her.
But my father was in such an emotional condition, he couldn't hide it from his mother.
And she realized that there is something very wrong.
So we had to tell this old lady-- she was 81 years old-- that her son had died and she had to leave, you know?
I mean, it was an incredible week in our family.
Incredible.
And where did she go?
Do you know?
She went-- she was taken to Theresienstadt.
And she lived there until January 22, 1943.
A-- a cousin of hers send us a card from Theresienstadt, which I still have, to inform us of her death.
His family.
Yes, his youngest brother, Alfred, the musician, and his family.
He had two little girls, born in '34 and '39.

And he bought false papers and-- Danish papers, Danish passports-- and went underground, and traveled to the city of Hamburg, and lived there as Danish citizens, registered with the police, and got regular ration cards, and even mailed some to us so we would have-- and-- and-- and he was working and everything was fine.
And-- and Lucy, the oldest one, who had to go to school, was instructed not to mention who she really is.
And everything went fine until February 27, 1943.
So he was underground only for about six or eight weeks.
It was a very short period.
And on February 27, 1943, when they arrested all the Jews in Berlin in the factories, they arrested him and his family in Hamburg.
And we learned later that the false papers he had bought for 15,000 marks were sold by the Gestapo.
And the Gestapo knew exactly where these people were who bought these false papers.
He and his family were brought back to Berlin.
And one day, we learned, during that period-- we received a postcard written by Lucy, the oldest, eight years old.
Dear Uncle and Aunt, we are back in Berlin.
We are in the Gestapo prison in such-and-such.
You can come and visit us.
And my father and I right away went there.
And they-- they let us see the children.
It was also in that same prison connected to the Jewish hospital.
And they let us see the children.
And they even gave us permission to take those two children home for one day, and we did.
And we tried-- and-- and then we learned that my uncle was in the police prison in-- in Berlin and my-- my aunt was in a woman's prison in Berlin-- not Gestapo prison, regular prisons.
And we tried to talk to the oldest, especially, to tell her, you will be reunited with mom and dad.
And-- and she said, I don't want to be reunited with them, because when they picked us up, they beat up dad.
And I don't want to be reunited with him again.
So she felt-- eight years old, but she felt that there was a danger to be with her parents, you know?
But they were all united again to be transported to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From Auschwitz, we received from my uncle an illegal letter, , which I do not have anymore because my father destroyed it out of fear.
He had smuggled that letter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of the camp with a-- a German worker who came into the camp to do I don't know what.
But he left the camp at night.
And fortunately, that-- that man was good enough to smuggle that letter out.

And in that letter, my uncle told us that on-- upon arrival, he was separated from his wife and the two girls, and he never saw them again, and that he is put to work-- very hard work in the beginning.
But at that time, when he wrote the letter, he was better off because he was in the orchestra.
And there was an-- an-- on-- in Auschwitz was an orchestra that had to play every morning and every night.
And he was in that orchestra.
But he also died.
He lived until January of '44, which I learned later from somebody who survived Auschwitz and who was together with him, and who had known him from the earlier years.
So we had authentic report of his demise, if you want to call it that way.
He died.
He was not gassed.
He died, as far as I know.
And your husband-to-be, you mentioned also, was underground at some point in Berlin.
Yes, yes.
They went-- the-- the family Winterfeldt-- his mother, his father, and he went underground on February 15, 1943-- lived underground with the help of all different kinds of people until June 12, 1944.
Two days prior to that, my ex-husband had met somebody who he had worked with.
This guy was working-- was a Jew, was working for the Gestapo, followed him, and gave his name away.
The Gestapo arrested my boyfriend at the time.
And of course, he had to say where his parents were.
And he-- they all went to Auschwitz in June of '44.
All three survived.
And they are the only German-Jewish family who survived.
Father, mother, and son went together and came back.
And he left then for America.
And much later, we got married.
Yes.
Right.
On-- on September 19, 1941 was the day when we all were very afraid.
What-- what will happen when we now walk out on the street with our stars?
And we were all watching.
Nothing happened.
Nothing happened.
And in the afternoon, I finally had the guts to go out.
Nothing happened.
Nothing.
We didn't go to school.
I mean, schools were closed out of fear that we children would be attacked.
Nothing happened.
Not once was my father or I or anybody I know in the city of Berlin attacked because he was wearing the star.
Not once.
And on that September 19, between 4:00 and 5:00, as I mentioned, you can go shopping.
I went to the bakery to buy bread, to get our bread.
And I walked into the store now with my star.
And the-- the owner, the woman, she just shook her head like this.
It's impossible.
And then she went in the back of the store and got her son, who was a soldier-- a German soldier on furlough.
And she-- I-- I-- I had known him from before, you know?
Saw him.
And he saw the star.
And he came and hugged me.
And he was wearing the uniform.
And he was hugging me, just to show me-- I-- I don't even know what happened in that moment.
He was so sad.
He was a young guy.
He must have been 21 or so.
And then he went behind the counter, and he packed bags with bread, and-- and rolls, and cake.
And I wasn't even allowed to pay.
And they gave that all to me and sent me home.
It was-- [?
Inglehart ?] was the name of these people.
See?
And you still would visit your grandfather in his barber shop--
Of course.
--wearing your star.
Wearing my star.
Had to come through the front door, of course.
Whether he-- all the customers he had for many, many years, they all knew.
They were all nice.
They all said hi to me, you know?
There was not--
No, no, no.
No, they didn't.
They didn't.
My-- my grandfather on his own was a daredevil, you know?
I mean, Hitler's birthday, all the stores were supposed to have in their display windows a picture of Hitler and-- and flowers.
What did he do?
He pulled the shades down and put a sign on-- closed because of sickness.
That was my grandfather.
Silent resistance, you know?
Yeah.
Well, that was her brother, who joined the party out of fear of losing his job.
But later on, I found out that he was quite convinced and loved his Fuhrer.
That was his business.
My mother never mentioned his name as long as she lived.
And there were no pictures in our family albums of him or anything.
He-- he just didn't exist.
But his wife, Gretel, who visited us behind his back to see how we're doing-- not that she could help us in any way, but she at least showed her loyalty to us.
She came to us during the night of the Crystal Night, you know, to see how we are doing.
And she kept always contact with us.
And they had a daughter, Gisela, who's younger than I am.
And my aunt-- my uncle was killed by the Russians in 1945.
And my aunt and my cousin, we-- we were very close.
And I'm still very close with my cousin.
She was just here now, visiting me.
And I-- we make it actually our business to see each other every year.
So she will come every year.
I will not travel anymore because I suffered a heart attack in Germany last year.
I don't want to go back.
Right, right.
Right.
I was invited by the city of Berlin, you know?
And the rededication of the Neue Synagoge, it's called, the New Synagogue-- that was the name of that synagogue in which my father was a cantor in the '30s, and in which my parents were married.
And I wrote an article for the book that was published simultaneously with the rededication of the temple.
And so they invited me.
And that was last year, last May. 50 years of liberation, yeah.
Right.
Right, yes.
I received a letter.
I had to report.
And I got there.
And I had a girlfriend-- also a girl from mixed-- a mixed marriage.
And she worked in this uniform repair shop.
And I-- when I got there to this Jewish employment office, I said, couldn't I work there?
Oh, yes.
Sure you can.
So I was also sent to that uniform repair shop.
It was more than a shop.
It was two big whole halls full of Jews, and Poles, and Russians, and Italians-- all forced labor workers.
And there, we had to repair German uniforms.
But we were treated well.
A certain Mr.
Hoch ?] was the foreman, so to speak he ran it.
And he treated everybody nice.
Everybody.
As a matter of fact, we were sitting there without a star, working, you know?
And-- and-- the-- the Gestapo would come and check on-- on-- on the setup of-- of this factory.
And-- and he would come running out, and put your stars on.
Do you have all your stars on?
The Gestapo is coming.
You know, he would warn us, you know?
He was very-- they were very decent.
And he treated the Russians very decent, which was-- they were the poorest of the poor, these Russian prisoners.
They lived in a camp, you know?
And they were brought to work every day.
They were so poor.
It was horrible.
What we shared with them what we had.
But he treated them well.
And after the war, the Russians saw to it that he had a good position.
He worked for the Russian army.
He was their tailor then, yeah.
He deserved every-- every good thing.
Yes.
We received 32 pfennig per hour.
And you had to-- you had to-- on this, you had to pay 15% Jew tax, on-- on top of all the other tax.
Nothing was left over.
Practically nothing was left over.
Who cares?
There was nothing to buy anyway, you know?
Money at that time didn't make any difference.
I worked the night shift, yeah.
I started at-- at--
12 hours, yes.
I started-- from 7:00 to 7:00, I worked.
Well, I was very handy.
My grandma Jenny and also my other grandma were both very, very handy.
And since Grandma Jenny lived with us, I had learned a lot from her.
And I'm-- I'm handy.
And I did all right there, yeah.
Well, I-- I would estimate about 1,000 or so-- all the Jewish partners of-- of these mixed marriages and their Jewish children.
Maybe 1,200.
I'm not sure.
At that time, we didn't wear the star anymore.
We discarded the star.
How did that happen?
One day, Hermann Goering got on the-- on-- on the radio and said, now Berlin is free of Jews.
Now, Berlin is free of Jews, and we are still running around with the star.
So slowly, one by one, dared-- first we-- I would wear a-- a-- cover it with a pocketbook or any other object.
And then one day, you would go down without it, and [INAUDIBLE] without it.
Well, that was-- since I worked during the night, I was home during the day, all alone.
And every day in '44, we had air raids.
And when these air raids came, I went into the cellar.
And one day, I-- I thought I heard-- the sirens would announce the beginning of the air raid and the end.
And I was in the cellar and I-- I-- I somehow thought I heard the sirens going that all clear, you can come out of the cellar again.
And I went out.
And I went out on the street, and there was a policeman in uniform-- a policeman standing.
And he said, what are you doing out here?
Oh, I said, I thought it was over.
It was over.
No, it's not over.
What are you doing here?
Show me your identification.
Are you a spy?
Because the American flyers flew very low, you know?
You-- you could see the airplanes.
Are you a spy?
Show me your identification.
And I was hesitating and hesitating.
And I said, I'll go right back.
And he said, show me your identification.
He was-- got-- he got very harsh.
And I said, if I show you my identification, my father and I are finished because I am Jewish.
And he said, go back into that cellar and close the door.
That's all.
See, if he would have been a different man, he would have, yes, reported us, and my father and I-- my mother couldn't have done anything for us at that point, you know?
First of all, I was wearing a star.
I was out there conspiring with the airplanes.
We would have been lost.
Now, what did you know in Berlin about the progress of the war?
Oh, we-- we-- well, Jews were not allowed, of course, to have a radio.
We had no radio.
But my mother, courageous as-- as she was, applied on her own for a radio-- permission to have a radio.
And she got the permission without any questions.
It was toward the end of the war.
Everything was a little upside down already.
And she got the permission.
And she got a radio from-- from Gretel, you know?
That-- her-- her-- her brother's wife.
She gave her a-- a radio.
And this was a good radio.
We heard BBC run.
And then we heard where the Allies were and what was doing, and-- as long as we had electricity, until that failed.
But it failed very late.
Now, in Berlin, by, say, late '44 or '45, did you have any idea of what had happened to all the people who were deported?
Or was this still a secret?
We had no idea.
No idea.
No idea.
I don't even think I-- I-- I had no idea.

The only thing that made me suspicious-- but I never said it, never voiced it-- was that my uncle wrote in this illegal letter that he-- after arrival in Auschwitz, that he never saw his wife and his daughters again.
And that made me suspicious.
What did they do to them?
But I never voiced it, you know?
No.
Would you-- we couldn't imagine.
We-- we had so much-- when-- when the announcement-- BBC announced in '45 Buchenwald was liberated, I said, oh, they're all coming home.
That was my reaction.
See?
We didn't know.
We didn't.
This was beyond human imagination.
We couldn't.
Now, going into '45, at that point, your biggest physical danger was probably the air raids.
Yeah.
Well--
--we took them in stride.
I-- I-- I-- the more the better.
Every house I saw burning, as long as it wasn't our house, I was happy.
People were moaning and groaning, they're losing everything and everything's burning.
I said, good.
[LAUGHS]
Oh-- oh, they were-- they were just as happy as we were.
It had to come to an end.
That-- that-- the sad part was that my grandfather lost his life in one of the air raids.
And in '45, he burned alive, you know?
And that was very hard for us to take, yeah.
He was in-- he was in his cellar.
But then they had to leave the cellar.
He went back into the barber shop to save his stamp collection.
And that was the end.
And my grandma got out.
And-- and-- and she survived that air raid, but not grandpa.
No, no.
Not-- we knew it at-- in the-- during the last 10 days, I would say.
Oh, yeah.
There were terrible propaganda against the Russians, what they would do, and-- and rob everybody, rape all the women, and so on and so forth.
Of course, we didn't believe that.
And we were not fearful of the Russians at all, my parents and I.
But when they came, it was a different story, you know?
The--
The last few days.
Right.
I was still doing forced labor.
And on April 21, the artillery started shooting into the inner city of Berlin.
I said, I'm not gonna go to work tonight anymore.
That's it.
Finished.
And I didn't go.
And my father and myself moved a few things into the cellar, because my father said, this will take a while.
And we cannot stay in the apartment.
We have to be in the cellar away from the artillery.
My mother, on the other hand, went into, for years, a public bunker, which was very deep in the ground.
First of all, she was very fearful of the bombs.
But she said, if I die, what will happen to you?
I have to be safe.
So she went into-- she was in the bunker when artillery started.
Were you allowed in there, or you weren't allowed?
We-- we-- we wouldn't go in there.
We--
Yeah, we wouldn't go in there.
And you see, you had-- don't-- don't forget, my father was a man in his late 40's.
He should have been in the army.
He avoided any contact with any German authorities.
Yeah, no.
No, we wouldn't go in the bunker, never.
And-- but-- when-- as I said, on the 21st of April, artillery started.
And in-- during that evening, we still were able to hear BBC.
And BBC said that there will be no more air raids because the Russians are too close to Berlin.
And of course, the Americans didn't want to bomb the-- the Russian army.
And at that point, my father said, now I'm gonna go to the bunker and get mom out.
And he did, and she came.
So then we were all three together in the cellar.
Slowly and slowly, more and more people came out of the bunker because the bunker was horrible.
I mean, it took-- it took nine days or-- or-- or eight days, you know, until it was all over.
The fighting in the inner city was horrible in Berlin.
So I was sitting in the cellar, and waiting, and waiting.
And then the electricity went, and we had no radio.
And, well, we just waited.
What happened, what happened, you know?
Did you have some food with you?
We had some food.
And there was a restaurant in-- in-- in the same building.
And this woman, all of a sudden, pulled out of her-- her own private cellar all kinds of food.
And we ate.
You know, we had to eat.
We-- we-- they shared with us, you know?
It was OK.
It was OK then.
And then one day, my father went-- my father went outside.
And he always looked.
And [INAUDIBLE] said, the Russians are here.
They're in our street.
I saw the first Russian soldiers.
And then he came back down in the cellar, and we waited.
And then there was rattling and knocking.
And the Russians came.
My father opened the door with the first Russian soldier.
And my father knew some Russian, spoke to him, told him who we were, who he is, and that his family is there.
And this Russian soldier was also Jewish, and they embraced.
And he-- the-- this Russian soldier right away informed his commander that there's a Jewish family.
And the commander came, who was a tall, handsome guy.
A-- a-- a Tartar?
An Asian type.
I don't know, actually, where he was from.
It doesn't matter.
And he right away sorted the cellar.
Now, 3/4 of the cellar was for the Jewish family, and the others were all pushed in one tiny little corner, you know?
And he was tough on them.
We got food.
He gave us water from-- from their army supplies, you know?
Saw to it that we are safe.
He watched me like a hawk, because soldiers rape women.
And I was 17 years old, you know?
So he watched me like a hawk.
I mean, I really have to thank him that nothing happened to me.
That lasted about 24 hours.
And the fighting was still fierce.
They hadn't-- they couldn't progress.
And then we had to all out into the courtyard of the building.
I mean, the building was half destroyed by now.
And we were all lined up in the courtyard, and they put up machine guns.
And I don't know.
I said, there goes my young life.
I was ready--
